passages.explanation: The whole assembly rises and leads Jesus to Pilate. They begin accusing him: we've found this man subverting our nation. He opposes payment of taxes to Caesar and claims to be Messiah, a king. Pilate asks: are you the king of the Jews? You have said so, Jesus replies. Pilate tells the chief priests and the crowd: I find no basis for a charge against this man. But they insist: he stirs up the people all over Judea by his teaching. He started in Galilee and has come all the way here. The charges are reframed for a Roman audience — religious blasphemy becomes political sedition. The tax accusation is a direct lie; Jesus told them to pay Caesar's taxes. Pilate sees through it immediately.

When Pilate hears mention of Galilee, he asks if the man is a Galilean. Learning he's under Herod's jurisdiction, he sends him to Herod, who is also in Jerusalem at that time. Herod is greatly pleased — he's been wanting to see Jesus for a long time and hopes to see a miracle. He plies Jesus with many questions, but Jesus gives him no answer. The chief priests and teachers of the law stand there, vehemently accusing him. Herod and his soldiers ridicule and mock Jesus. They dress him in an elegant robe and send him back to Pilate. That day Herod and Pilate become friends — before this they'd been enemies. Jesus is silent before Herod — the man who killed John the Baptist and wanted to see a magic trick doesn't deserve a response. The political reconciliation between Herod and Pilate over Jesus' body is darkly ironic: enemies unite against the Prince of Peace.

Pilate calls together the chief priests, the rulers, and the people. He tells them: you brought me this man as one who was inciting the people to rebellion. I've examined him and found no basis for your charges. Neither has Herod — he sent him back to us. He has done nothing to deserve death. Therefore I'll punish him and release him. But the whole crowd shouts: away with this man! Release Barabbas to us! Barabbas has been thrown in prison for an insurrection in the city and for murder. Pilate appeals to them again, wanting to release Jesus. But they keep shouting: crucify him! Crucify him! A third time he speaks: why? What crime has this man committed? I've found no grounds for the death penalty. I'll have him punished and released. But with loud shouts they insistently demand that he be crucified, and their shouts prevail. Pilate decides to grant their demand. He releases Barabbas — the man imprisoned for insurrection and murder — and surrenders Jesus to their will. Three times Pilate declares Jesus innocent. Three times the crowd overrules him. The actual insurrectionist goes free while the innocent man takes his place.

As the soldiers lead Jesus away, they seize Simon from Cyrene, who is on his way in from the country, and put the cross on him to carry behind Jesus. A large number of people follow, including women who mourn and wail for him. Jesus turns and says: daughters of Jerusalem, don't weep for me — weep for yourselves and your children. For the time will come when you'll say 'blessed are the childless women, the wombs that never bore and the breasts that never nursed.' Then they'll say to the mountains 'fall on us' and to the hills 'cover us.' For if people do these things when the tree is green, what will happen when it is dry? On the way to his own execution, Jesus is warning Jerusalem about its future destruction. If Rome does this to an innocent man in peacetime, what will they do to a guilty city in wartime?

Two other men, both criminals, are led out to be executed with Jesus. When they arrive at the place called the Skull, they crucify him there along with the criminals — one on his right, the other on his left. Jesus says: Father, forgive them, for they don't know what they are doing. They divide up his clothes by casting lots. The people stand watching. The rulers sneer: he saved others — let him save himself if he's God's Messiah, the Chosen One. The soldiers also mock him, offering him wine vinegar: if you're the king of the Jews, save yourself. There's a written notice above him: this is the king of the Jews. One of the criminals hurls insults: aren't you the Messiah? Save yourself and us! But the other criminal rebukes him: don't you fear God, since you're under the same sentence? We're punished justly — we're getting what our deeds deserve. But this man has done nothing wrong. Then he says: Jesus, remember me when you come into your kingdom. Jesus answers: truly I tell you, today you will be with me in paradise. Jesus' first words from the cross are a prayer for his executioners. His last significant interaction before death is with a criminal who has nothing to offer — no good works, no time left, just a request to be remembered. And Jesus promises him everything.

It's about noon, and darkness comes over the whole land until three in the afternoon — the sun stops shining. The curtain of the temple is torn in two. Jesus calls out with a loud voice: Father, into your hands I commit my spirit. When he says this, he breathes his last. The centurion, seeing what happens, praises God: surely this was a righteous man. All the people who've gathered to witness this sight beat their breasts and go away. But all those who knew him, including the women who had followed him from Galilee, stand at a distance watching. Luke's account of Jesus' death is distinctive. The final words aren't a cry of abandonment (as in Mark and Matthew) but a prayer of trust — Psalm 31:5. The temple curtain tears, removing the barrier between God and humanity. The centurion's verdict matches Pilate's: this man was innocent.

A man named Joseph, a member of the Council, a good and upright man who had not consented to their decision and action, comes from the Judean town of Arimathea. He's been waiting for the kingdom of God. He goes to Pilate and asks for Jesus' body. He takes it down, wraps it in linen cloth, and places it in a tomb cut in the rock, where no one has yet been laid. It's Preparation Day, and the Sabbath is about to begin. The women who've come with Jesus from Galilee follow Joseph and see the tomb and how the body is laid. Then they go home and prepare spices and perfumes. But they rest on the Sabbath in obedience to the commandment. Joseph risks everything — as a Council member, publicly claiming the body of an executed criminal marks him permanently. The women note the location carefully. They intend to come back.
